title: Question: Ignoring %Error in not "generated" Verilog input.
body:
I'm just getting started using Verilator, so sorry if this is too much of a noob question.  BTW, thanks for yet another great tool!

I have "sanity checks" in various places in my Verilog, filling in for lack of SV asserts.  It goes like this:
```
   // Synthesis-time sanity Check
   generate
      if(FREQ_CLK_SAMPLE != 4*FREQ_CLK)
        __error__("DDRx4 Requires Edge Clock 4x the Parallel Clock.");
   endgenerate
```
The intention is to cause a show-stopper when this module is instantiated incorrectly so I don't chase my tail later.
Various synthesis tools and simulators haven't complained when the parameters are correct.  Verilator throws this error:
```
$ verilator --default-language "1364-2001" -F ./modulepathflags.vc --lint-only --Wall input_ddrx4_machxo3.v 
%Error: input_ddrx4_machxo3.v:19:18: syntax error, unexpected '(', expecting IDENTIFIER or randomize
   19 |         __error__("DDRx4 Requires Edge Clock 4x the Parallel Clock.");
      |                  ^
%Error: Exiting due to 1 error(s)
```

I traced the meaning down thanks to test cases for Issue468.  I see a way I could fix them for Verilator:  make a real module __error__ and don't use it anonymously.

Here is the question part: Is there an easier short-cut out of this?  Like telling Verilator "don't dive into unused generate branches?"
